title: Trust Through Shared Secrets
concept: When two parties share information only they can decode, they establish a secure channel where eavesdroppers see noise—trust doesn't require trusting the infrastructure.

questions.question: What makes a lock-and-key system secure even when someone controls the road between sender and receiver?
options: The message travels too fast to intercept
Only the two endpoints hold keys that can decode the message
The company running the service verifies each message
questions.explanation: Security comes from mathematical keys at the endpoints, not from trusting the infrastructure. Even if someone controls every server the message passes through, they see scrambled data—only the recipient's key can unscramble it.

questions.question: Why does a message downgrade to unprotected when two systems speak different languages?
options: Older technology is inherently more secure
They fall back to the oldest common standard, which has no protection
Network speed drops when protocols don't match
Users must manually disable security features
questions.explanation: When systems don't share a secure protocol, they revert to the lowest common denominator—often a decades-old standard with no encryption. The fallback prioritizes message delivery over protection.

questions.question: A company can build secure bridges between competing platforms but chooses not to. What non-technical factor drives this?
options: Users prefer visible differences between platforms
Keeping the other side less secure creates switching costs
Technical standards take decades to develop
questions.explanation: When one platform offers features the other can't match, switching means losing those features—friends, photos, security. This friction keeps users locked in. The technical capability existed for years; the business incentive to build it didn't.
